visits.text: Hike to Colombier Beach — Start from the Petite Anse parking area and take the coastal trail. 30 minutes walking through tropical vegetation with sea views. The path descends to Colombier beach, an isolated cove accessible only on foot or by boat. Protected marine reserve with crystal-clear water, white sand surrounded by hills.
Colombier trail access — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Petite Anse — Small beach at the start of the Colombier trail. Less crowded than major beaches, calm water perfect for swimming. Rocky coastline on each side creates a protected bay. Several parking spaces under trees. Good starting point for coastal exploration.
Cliff viewpoints — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Flamands Beach — Long crescent beach with white sand bordered by latania palms. Popular with families for its calm waters and gentle slope. Several restaurants and bars along the beach. Beautiful sunset views with the sun sinking into the Caribbean Sea. Relaxed and authentic atmosphere.
Turtle meadows — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Corossol Village — Traditional fishing village preserving the island's old way of life. Elderly women still wear traditional dress: white bonnets called quichenottes, long dresses. Small beach with colorful fishing boats. Browse stalls selling hats and baskets woven from latania palm by local craftswomen.
NW promontories — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Inter Oceans Museum — Small private museum displaying over 9,000 shells collected worldwide by founder Ingénu Magras. Impressive collection of rare specimens, fossils, and semi-precious stones. Educational panels on marine biology and Caribbean ecosystems. Located in Corossol.
Hidden coves — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Public beach — Small public beach near Corossol with shaded trees and picnic tables. Local atmosphere, few tourists. Calm water protected from waves. Free parking and facilities. Perfect spot to observe authentic island life away from luxury resorts.
Ridge track — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

activities.text: Full day at Colombier Beach — After the morning hike, spend the day at this isolated beach. Snorkel along the rocks; the protected marine reserve shelters sea turtles, rays, and tropical fish. Bring a picnic and drinks as there are no facilities. Enjoy the peaceful atmosphere of this preserved cove.

Sunset at Flamands Beach — End the day at this west-facing beach to watch the sunset. The sun sinks directly into the sea creating spectacular colors. Quiet atmosphere with local families enjoying the evening. Perfect spot to relax after the Colombier hike.

Latania palm craft workshop — Meet Corossol women perpetuating traditional weaving techniques. Watch them create hats, bags, and baskets from dried and woven latania fronds. Purchase unique handmade souvenirs while supporting local craftswomen. Fascinating cultural experience.

Traditional fishing and local culture — Chat with fishermen on the beach about traditional fishing methods still used today. Observe the colorful saintois boats, traditional Caribbean fishing vessels. Learn the island's history and how locals maintain their culture despite surrounding development.

days_plan.itin_title: Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons

visits.text: Grand Cul-de-Sac Lagoon — Shallow lagoon protected by an outer reef creating calm turquoise waters. Popular spot for kitesurfing and windsurfing with steady trade winds. The reef blocks waves while winds accelerate over the water. Beach lined with latania palms, sea grapes, and beach bars.
Grand Cul-de-Sac lagoon — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Natural pools and tide exploration — Walk the beach to discover natural rock pools filled by high tide. Observe small fish, crabs, and sea urchins trapped in these natural aquariums. The barrier reef creates calm zones perfect for families with young children. Clear shallow water with sandy bottom.
Reef flats — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Marigot Beach — Small quiet cove just before Grand Cul-de-Sac. Less crowded than the main beach, perfect for those seeking calm. Crystal-clear water, good snorkeling along the rocks on each side. Shaded areas under trees, intimate and relaxed atmosphere.
Views over the point — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Fort Oscar — Swedish fortification from the 18th century protecting the west side of Gustavia harbor. The fort housed the governor's residence. Partially preserved stone ruins with visible foundations. Panoramic view over the harbor entrance and Corossol coast. Quiet spot to contemplate the island's history.
Mangrove edges — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

West Coast Scenic Route — Drive the narrow winding road along the west coast. Pass through small villages, cross green hills, discover isolated coves. Stop at viewpoints to admire the rugged coastline and turquoise waters. Authentic side of the island away from tourist centers.
Kayak launch — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Anse des Cayes — Small pebble beach at the island's northwest tip. Often deserted, wild atmosphere. Strong waves, rocky coast. Access via steep trail. Spot for those seeking isolation and raw natural beauty.
Reef platforms — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

activities.text: Kitesurfing or windsurfing lesson — Grand Cul-de-Sac is one of the Caribbean's best spots for these sports. Consistent trade winds, shallow water with sandy bottom, protected lagoon. Several schools offer beginner lessons and equipment rental for experienced riders. Stunning setting with white sand and turquoise water.

Stand-Up Paddling toward the reef — Rent a SUP to explore the lagoon and head toward the barrier reef. Paddle over shallow turquoise waters, observe the sandy bottom and seagrass beds beneath you. Spot sea turtles feeding in the lagoon. Peaceful and gentle activity perfect for families.

Complete island tour by car — Make a full circuit of the island stopping at all major viewpoints and villages. Rediscover previous days' locations with fresh perspective. Appreciate how compact the island is while each coast has its own character. Final synthesis of the stay.

Final beach and relaxation — Choose your favorite beach to spend the last full day. Whether the Grand Cul-de-Sac lagoon, lively St-Jean, or wild Saline. Savor the island's beauty one last time, reflect on discoveries made during the stay. Relaxed pace to end the trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons

visits.text: Gustavia Harbor — This U-shaped harbor was built in 1785 by the Swedes and named after King Gustav III. The free port welcomes luxury yachts and cruise ships in a protected natural bay. Watch the constant parade of exceptional vessels anchored in turquoise waters.
Harbor quays — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Wall House - Territorial Museum — This mysterious stone building has an enigmatic history: was it a theater, an officers' hotel, or a political hall? The ground floor houses a permanent exhibition on Saint Barthélemy's history. In the courtyard, discover the ruins of a stone and brick bread oven.
Wall House (exterior) — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Fort Gustav — Climb to the ruins of the most important fort from the Swedish period, built in 1787 on the foundations of a 17th-century French structure. The Gustavia lighthouse (1961) now dominates the site. The panoramic view over the harbor and neighboring islands of St. Eustatius and Saba is worth the climb under the Caribbean sun.
Fort Gustav viewpoint — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Fort Karl — Explore the remains of this 18th-century fortification overlooking Shell Beach south of Gustavia. The cannons pointed seaward to protect the free port entrance. Accessible hiking trail with spectacular views over the rugged coastline and turquoise waters.
Fort Karl path — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Shell Beach — Discover this unusual beach covered with millions of crushed shells rolled by the waves, resembling coarse pink sand. Notable origin: the dredging of Gustavia harbor in the 1960s-1970s deposited sand and shells in Grand Galet cove, creating this 1 km artificial beach. Impressive view of St. Eustatius and Saba islands offshore.
Shell Beach viewpoint — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Waterfront promenade — Walk along the colorful quays lined with restaurants, duty-free shops, and historic buildings. Swedish-era warehouses have been converted into upscale boutiques. Stop at café terraces to watch the yachts, sailboats, and constant harbor activity. Relaxed and elegant atmosphere typical of the island.
Lighthouse point — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Saline Beach — One of the island's most beautiful beaches, completely wild and undeveloped. Long stretch of fine white sand, natural dunes, lush vegetation at the back. No facilities, bars, or restaurants. 10-minute walk from the parking area through low vegetation. Crystal-clear water but sometimes strong waves.
Saline trail — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Gouverneur Beach — Spectacular crescent beach nestled between green hills. Access via a winding downhill road. White sand, turquoise waters, moderate waves. Often uncrowded despite its beauty. Breathtaking panorama from the elevated road. Peaceful and wild atmosphere.
Gouverneur hill — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Saline Pond — Wetland behind Saline beach, refuge for migratory birds. Spot herons, egrets, and frigatebirds in this preserved natural habitat. Walking trail bordering the pond. Important ecological site on the island.
Salt flats — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Lorient Beach — Long family-friendly beach with calm waters and gentle slope. Less crowded than St-Jean or Grand Cul-de-Sac. Several access points along the road. Mix of locals and tourists. Relaxed atmosphere, some nearby restaurants and small shops.
Old anchors — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Notre-Dame de l'Assomption Church — Small Catholic church of simple architecture overlooking Lorient. Built in the 19th century on the site of the island's first chapel. Mass celebrated in French and Creole. Peaceful cemetery with old headstones bearing Swedish and French names.
Customs house (exterior) — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Pointe Milou — Rocky promontory east of Lorient offering coastal views. Luxury villas built on the cliffs. Scenic road along the coast with ocean views. Powerful waves crashing against volcanic rocks. Popular spot for sunrise photos.
Quay commemorative plaques — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

Sunset from the harbor — Find a spot on Gustavia's quays to watch the daily spectacle of the sun setting behind the island's hills. The U-shaped harbor lights up with golden then orange reflections. Luxury yacht silhouettes cut against the fiery backdrop. Magical atmosphere perfectly ending a Saint Barth stay.
Sunset over harbor — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Historic quays — Take a final stroll along the restored quays bordering the free port. Admire colorful Creole house architecture with galleries, historic anchors on display, commemorative plaques telling stories of famous explorers and navigators. Soak up the unique atmosphere blending Swedish heritage and French Caribbean culture.
Quays — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Farewell walk — Wander the cobbled streets of the historic center for a final exploration. Pass the Catholic and Anglican churches, Wall House, Rétrocession Square. Browse upscale duty-free shops. Savor a last cocktail on a terrace overlooking the harbor before leaving this Caribbean gem.
Farewell walk — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

activities.text: Fort Gustav-Lighthouse urban hike — Climb to the lighthouse located at the former Fort Gustav site. Exceptional panorama over the harbor, town, and neighboring islands. Well-maintained trail with stairs, easy path but exposed to sun.

Historic downtown walking tour — Stroll through the capital's streets to discover Swedish-influenced architecture: wooden houses with ornate balconies, typical galleries. Walk along Samuel Fahlberg Street, Vanadis Square with its Neptune's trident, and Rétrocession Square commemorating the return to France in 1877.

Kayaking or Stand-Up Paddling in the harbor — Rent equipment at the quays to explore the bay from the water. Paddle among anchored yachts, approach the historic forts, discover hidden coves along Shell Beach. Calm and clear water, activity suitable for all levels.

Sunset hike to Fort Karl — Take the coastal trail from Shell Beach to Fort Karl ruins during golden hour. Climb to the ramparts as the setting sun illuminates the fortifications and harbor. Magical view of the town bathed in warm light. Bring your camera for spectacular photos.

Full day at Saline Beach — Spend the day at this pristine beach. Bring everything you need: water, food, umbrella, sun protection. Swim in the waves (watch for currents), walk the endless white sand, admire the wild beauty. Peaceful atmosphere far from tourist crowds.

Sunset at Gouverneur Beach — End the day at this west-facing beach for a spectacular sunset. The sun hides behind the hills creating dramatic lighting. Often deserted at this hour. Romantic and wild setting perfect for evening contemplation.

Surfing at Lorient Beach — When conditions are right, Lorient receives beautiful waves suitable for beginner and intermediate surfers. More consistent swell than in protected bays. Local surf shops rent boards and offer lessons. Friendly surf vibe, popular among island residents.

Coastal route and viewpoint tour — Drive the scenic coastal road from Lorient to Toiny. Stop at several viewpoints to admire the wild Atlantic-facing coast. Spectacular cliffs, powerful waves, isolated coves. Beautiful scenic route with photo opportunities at every turn.

Gourmet dinner overlooking the harbor — Reserve a table at one of the acclaimed restaurants lining Gustavia harbor. Refined French cuisine or revisited Creole specialties by talented chefs. Savor grilled lobster, fresh catch of the day, tropical desserts while watching the nighttime ballet of illuminated yachts. Elegant and relaxed atmosphere typical of St Barth lifestyle.

Last-minute shopping — Take advantage of the free port status for duty-free shopping before departure. Luxury boutiques offering perfumes, cosmetics, jewelry, watches, designer clothing at attractive prices. Art galleries exhibiting Caribbean artists' works. Local crafts: woven latania hats from Corossol, spiced rums, natural cosmetics.

days_plan.itin_title: Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons

visits.text: St-Jean Beach — This beach is divided in two by the Eden Rock promontory. Watch planes land just meters above your head on the 646-meter runway, ranked as the world's third most dangerous airport. White sand, turquoise water, beach clubs and restaurants. Perfect for swimming with gentle waves most of the year.
Views over St-Jean bay — Monument featuring a brief historical overview of the island.

Gustaf III Airport observation — Position yourself at the end of St-Jean beach or at the Remy de Haenen viewpoint to watch plane landings. Aircraft descend over the bay, fly over the beach at just meters, and touch down on the short runway bordered by hills. Daily spectacle of small planes and jets maneuvering in this confined space.
Airport hill — Natural viewpoint; best light toward sunset.

Eden Rock — This luxury hotel stands on a rocky promontory dividing St-Jean beach. Even if you're not staying there, you can admire the impressive architecture and location. The rock serves as a natural jumping point. Snorkel around the formation to see tropical fish in crystal-clear waters.
Shopping arcade facades — Heritage element with interpretive panels.

activities.text: Snorkeling in St-Jean bay — Rent mask and fins on the beach to explore shallow reefs. Swim around Eden Rock and along the rocky coastline. Spot parrotfish, angelfish, and sergeant majors in their natural habitat. Clear water with excellent visibility year-round.

Beach clubs and local cuisine — Try the famous beach clubs lining St-Jean. Relaxed atmosphere with your feet in the sand, French and Creole cuisine, fresh seafood. Savor grilled lobster, local fish, tropical cocktails while watching planes land. Chic laid-back atmosphere typical of Saint Barth.

days_plan.itin_title: Exploring Saint Barthélemy: From Gustavia Harbor to the Eastern Lagoons
